coredata.dc:title: Design of polarity-dependence orange emission multifunctional carbon dots for water detection and anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Y.
Cui, S.
Lei, P.
Guo, J.
Wang, Q.
Shuang, S.
Dong, C.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, polarity-dependence orange emission multifunctional carbon dots (o-CDs) were designed for water detection in organic solvents and anti-counterfeiting. The o-CDs were readily fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal method with citric acid and p-aminoazobenzene as precursors. The o-CDs possess bright orange fluorescence, good photochemical stability, and polarity-dependence characteristic. With the increase of polarity, the emission peak of o-CDs gradually red-shifted. Meanwhile, with the increase of water content in organic solvent, the fluorescence intensity of o-CDs decreased significantly and the emission peak position gradually red-shifted. This is due to the specific water-fluorophore interaction (particularly hydrogen bond effects), part of o-CDs gathered, making the carbonized cores suffer from π–π stacking and reduced the transition energy gap. Based on this, a fluorescence sensing platform with satisfactory recoveries and relative standard deviation was constructed to detect water content in real alcohol solvents. Furthermore, the o-CDs were further designed into fluorescent ink and prepared fluorescent flexible films, realizing information encryption and solid-state fluorescence. This work opens up a possibility for the development of polarity-dependence orange emission nanomaterial based on CDs for water detection, anti-counterfeiting, and solid-state fluorescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Orange emission
Water detection
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of water in organic solvent
Preparation of test strips for humidity detection and test paper for writing
Photostability of o-CDs at different conditions
Detection of water content in organic solvents
Water content determination in real samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274090), the Graduate Student Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (No. 2021Y036).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset of HRTEM image); (b) size distribution; (c) 2D AFM image; (d) 3D AFM image of o-CDs. AFM, atomic force microscope; HRTEM, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy; TEM, transmission electron microscopy.
(a) XPS spectra of o-CDs; the high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) FTIR spectroscopy. FTIR, Fourier transform infrared; XPS, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy.

(a) The UV–visible absorption and excitation-emission spectrum; (b) and (c) excitation independent fluorescence spectra of o-CDs dispersed in water and ethanol, respectively; (d) the emission spectra of o-CDs in H2O, MeOH, EtOH, acetone, and THF. CD, carbon dot; UV, ultraviolet.

(a) Fluorescence spectra and (b) the broken line diagram of fluorescence intensity of o-CDs at different pH; (c) effect of time on fluorescence intensity; (d) effect of salt solution concentration on the relative fluorescence intensity. CD, carbon dot.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of o-CDs upon adding different water content into ethanol; (b) the fluorescence intensity versus water content; (c) the normalized graph of o-CDs upon adding different water content into ethanol; (d) the position of emission peak versus water content. CD, carbon dot.
Test strips of different air humidity are exposed to sunlight and UV light. UV, ultraviolet.
Applications as (a) fluorescent ink and (b) repeatable writing test paper.
Determination of water content in Shanxi Fen Wine.

head.ce:title: Design of polarity-dependence orange emission multifunctional carbon dots for water detection and anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Department, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan, 030008, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Department, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan, 030008, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, polarity-dependence orange emission multifunctional carbon dots (o-CDs) were designed for water detection in organic solvents and anti-counterfeiting. The o-CDs were readily fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal method with citric acid and p-aminoazobenzene as precursors. The o-CDs possess bright orange fluorescence, good photochemical stability, and polarity-dependence characteristic. With the increase of polarity, the emission peak of o-CDs gradually red-shifted. Meanwhile, with the increase of water content in organic solvent, the fluorescence intensity of o-CDs decreased significantly and the emission peak position gradually red-shifted. This is due to the specific water-fluorophore interaction (particularly hydrogen bond effects), part of o-CDs gathered, making the carbonized cores suffer from π–π stacking and reduced the transition energy gap. Based on this, a fluorescence sensing platform with satisfactory recoveries and relative standard deviation was constructed to detect water content in real alcohol solvents. Furthermore, the o-CDs were further designed into fluorescent ink and prepared fluorescent flexible films, realizing information encryption and solid-state fluorescence. This work opens up a possibility for the development of polarity-dependence orange emission nanomaterial based on CDs for water detection, anti-counterfeiting, and solid-state fluorescence.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is generally considered as one of the most significant components in living things and ecosystem, which, on the other hand, has also been regarded as a common impurity in organic solvents, affecting many technological applications such as environmental monitoring, clinical diagnosis, and food processing [1,2]. Many technical applications, such as chemicals storage and transportation and quality certification processes, are sensitive to moisture content. High quality products require the detection of organic solvents and atmospheric moisture content to be timely, sensitive, and convenient. Different detection techniques such as chromatography [3–5], electrochemistry [6], and Karl Fisher method [7] are applied for measuring water content. These measurement methods have the advantages of high-throughput screening and great accuracy. Nevertheless, some of them present non-negligible drawbacks such as sample pretreatment-complex, time-consuming, and sophisticated instruments-requiring. Fluorometric assay, as an alternative method, has received variety attention because of its simple operation, high excellent sensitivity, and convenient on-site detection [8,9]. To date, numerous fluorescent probes-based sensing materials including organic dye [10], copper complexes [11], metal-organic frameworks [12–14], and covalent organic frameworks [15,16] have been developed for to detection of water in various organic solvents. Unfortunately, these methods often require complex synthesis steps and cause toxicity to the organism and the environment so that they cannot meet the requirements of practical applications. Therefore, it is very necessary to develop simple and environment-friendly treatments for water detection.

Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging photoluminescence nanomaterial, is characterized by simple preparation, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and easy functionalization. It has shown tremendous potential in the field of sensors [17–19], photoelectronic devices [20], electromagnetic fillers [21], biomedicine [22,23], and anti-counterfeiting [24]. Especially, a great deal of effort has been made in water sensing. For instance, Wang et al. [25] firstly prepared CD–imidazole nanoprobe through covalently conjugating imidazole group onto the surface of CDs for water quantitative detection via the suppression of photoinduced electron transfer. Inspired by this, Chao et al. [26] synthesized CDs without integrating any additional materials and Wei et al. [27] obtained bare -CDs directly for the detection of water content in organic solvent. Subsequently, Arshad et al. [28] further synthesized aggregation-induced emission CDs as ‘turn-on’ fluorescent probe for moisture sensors in commercial products and drugs. Intriguingly, Wen et al. [29] developed convenient CuNCs-CDs-based test paper for visual humidity detection. Despite the excellent achievements [30–33], it is still a challenge to construct water-sensitive CDs, especially long wavelength CDs with comprehensive performances, such as high accuracy, wide linear detection range, low detection limit (LOD), fast response and high reusability, and so on. Thus, the development of high-selectivity, real-time, and great-efficiency probe used for water determination is critical and highly desirable.

Herein, we designed a facile synthesis of o-CDs) with polarity-dependence using citric acid and p-amino-azobenzene as precursors. Due to hydrogen bond-induced aggregation and other specific water-fluorophore interaction, the conjugation degree of the o-CDs system is increased, which reduced the emission energy level and promoted the probability of non-radiative transition, leading to the fluorescence quenching obviously and the optimum emission peak red-shifted. Thus, the double fluorescence sensing strategy based on o-CDs was demonstrated for more accurate quantification of water content in organic solvents. And, the o-CDs were converted into test strips for convenient, real-time, and visible humidity sensing. Furthermore, using acid and base as encryption and decryption agents, fluorescent ink, repeatable writing test paper, and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite film with o-CDs can be manufactured. This further expands the multiple applications of o-CDs in information security and solid-state luminescence.

First, o-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method as follows: 38.4 mg citric acid and 39.4 mg p-aminoazobenzene were dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water in a beaker, fully stirred magnetically, and ultrasonized to make them sufficiently dissolved. And then, the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL stainless steel Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. At the end of the reaction, it was cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, the raw samples were handled by centrifugation at 8500 rpm for 30 min and filtered with 0.2 μm filter membrane in order to remove larger solid impurities. Finally, the purified solid o-CDs powder after freeze-dried could be obtained for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water in organic solvent
ce:section.ce:para: Solution of different water contents (from 0.5 to 100%, v/v) are prepared by adjusting the ratio of water and ethanol. The o-CDs powder is added and dissolved to it, and then, the fluorescence spectrum at the optimum excitation wavelength was measured, respectively. Similar procedures were performed for water sensing in tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone, and methanol. All relevant measurements were repeated three times to ensure the accuracy of the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of test strips for humidity detection and test paper for writing
ce:section.ce:para: By soaking the ordinary filter paper in o-CDs solution overnight and drying in the air at room temperature, orange paper was acquired. Test paper served as an invisible instrument for writing. On the other hand, test paper was cut out as test strips for RH detection. The test strips are fixed in a sealed glass bottle. The air relative humidity (RH) is controlled by adjusting the balance ratio of water vapor with a saturated salt solution of LiCl, MgCl2, Mg (NO3)2, NaCl, KCl, and KNO3 at room temperature 20 °C. Their salt solution can obtain air humidity of 11.31%, 33.07%, 59.14%, 67.00%, 85.11%, and 98.00%, respectively.

1.0 g of PVA was added in 10 mL of ultrapure water, heated, and stirred at 80 °C until dissolved. The PVA solution was cooled down to room temperature slightly, and 10 μL of o-CDs was evenly mixed into it. Then, the uniform o-CDs/PVA mixture solution was pour into letter mold and dried in an oven under 45 °C for 6 h. Finally, o-CDs/PVA composite film was obtained successfully after the demolding.

To investigate the morphology and size of o-CDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM) was performed. Fig. 1 a shows o-CDs are well-dispersed spherical nanoparticles. The lattice spacing of o-CDs was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite. From Fig. 1b (statistical analysis of the particle size of o-CDs), we can see that o-CDs were mostly distributed in the range of 3.0–6.0 nm with the average diameter of 4.44 nm. The AFM image (Fig. 1c) and the height profile along the corresponding AFM line (Fig. 1c) revealed that o-CDs were similar particle heights of 4.62 ± 0.32 nm. Combined with TEM results, the particle size distribution of o-CDs was uniform and spheroidicity.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used for elemental species analysis and to characterize the chemical bonds and related electronic structure information contained on the surface of o-CDs. The XPS results showed three peaks centered at C1s (284.90 eV), O1s (400.45 eV), and N1s (521.75 eV). The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 2b) revealed that o-CDs could be decomposed into the peaks C–OH (288.35 eV), C–N (285.63 eV), and C–C/C=C (284.68 eV). The peak in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) indicates N exists mostly in the form of N–H (401.35 eV) and C–N (399.65 eV), respectively. The O1s spectrum of o-CDs deconvolutes to two peaks at 532.40 eV and 531.33 eV, which correspond to C–OH and C=O (Fig. S1). The surface functional groups of o-CDs also were identified using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). As shown in Fig. 2d, o-CDs exhibit peaks at around 3359.63 cm−1 and 3213.93 cm−1, which indicates the presence of the N–H wagging of amine and O–H stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1708.38 cm−1 and 1598.39 cm−1 were assigned to C=O and C=C stretching vibrations, respectively. Furthermore, the surface of o-CDs C=N (1511.26 cm−1), C–N (1399.84 cm−1), and aromatic C–NH (1234.15 cm−1) is observed.

Fig. 3 a demonstrated the ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of o-CDs. The UV–Vis absorption exhibited two characteristic absorption peaks at around 272 nm and 518 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic C=C bond and the n-π* transition of their surface functional groups. The optimum fluorescence excitation and emission peaks are centered at 540 and 601 nm. To further determine the optical properties, o-CDs were dispersed in water or organic solvents under the same conditions. Then, o-CDs solutions were excited at different excitation wavelength and the excitation-independent fluorescence behavior was widely observed, as shown in Figs. 3b, c and Fig. S2. As the excitation wavelength changed, the fluorescence intensity increased and then decreased. In addition, the insets of Figs. 3b and c showed o-CDs from dark orange in water to orange in ethanol under 365 nm UV lamp. Otherwise, the fluorescence emission maxima were located at 589 nm in methanol, 573 nm in ethanol, 565 nm in acetone, and 556 nm in THF, respectively (Fig. 3d). The emission wavelengths of o-CDs in different solvents were further verified through color coordinates. As shown in Fig. S3, the color coordinates of o-CDs in water, methanol, ethanol, acetone, and THF are (0.6344, 0.3652), (0.6211, 0.3765), (0.6177, 0.3818), (0.6063, 0.3932), and (0.6001, 0.3994), respectively. Reichardt's polarity parameter (E N T) is a normalized parameter that measures the overall solvation capability of solvents. As shown in Fig. S4, the emission peak position exhibits a good correlation with E N T. It indicates that the o-CDs possess different surface states and exhibits behavior of excitation wavelength variation due to changes in solvent polarity and intramolecular interactions. Take Rhodamine B as a standard, the fluorescence quantum yields of o-CDs were 1.75% in water, 13.35% in ethanol, 6.43% in methanol, 11.3% in acetone, and 18.56% in THF, respectively. What is more, Fig. S5 and Table S2 summarize the fluorescence lifetime of o-CDs in various solvents and it increases from 2.68 ns in water to 4.88 ns in ethanol, 4.39 ns in methanol, 5.74 ns in acetone, and 5.17 ns in THF. This phenomenon implies that the lifetime of o-CDs is generally longer in non-polar solvents than in polar solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability of o-CDs at different conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify that o-CDs are suitable for the next fluorescence experiment, the fluorescence spectrum under different pH of o-CDs is analyzed in Figs. 4 a and b. As the acidity gradually changes, the position of o-CDs fluorescence peak is basically unchanged and the excitation independence is still maintained. The fluorescence intensity of o-CDs increased first and then decreased. The fluorescence was stable at pH = 4–10 and the strongest was at pH = 8. Especially under acidic conditions, the degree of protonation is high and the hydrogen bond is enhanced, which makes the surface electronic structure of o-CDs change and the energy decreases. The picture below Fig. 4b was the color of different pH under 365 nm UV lamp. It was observed to be different in color because of the rearrangement of molecules during protonation and deprotonation. Different types of bodies have different structures and different absorption selectivity to light, resulting in different colors. This can be applied to designing an anti-counterfeit strip application. At the same time, Fig. 4c shows nearly constant intensity under 60 days (every 10 days), and o-CDs had good photostability. The effect of different concentration NaCl on the fluorescence intensity of o-CDs was investigated in Fig. 4d, revealing that the fluorescence intensity of o-CDs hardly changed significantly even under high ionic conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitative detection of water based on fluorescence chemical sensor is of great significance. The behavior of the o-CDs system for the water content detection in ethanol was assessed. With the increase of water content, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually and the position of the optimum peak red-shifted. The fluorescence spectrum in different water ratios were analyzed statistically, and excellent linear fitting relationship was achieved (Fig. 5 a). As a result, dual fluorometric sensing strategies of intensity and peak position can be constructed.

For the fluorescence intensity strategy, ΔF indicated a good linear relationship in the water content range from 0.5–15% to 15–100%, v/v (Fig. 5b). (ΔF = F0–F, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of o-CDs in ethanol and in the presence of water, respectively). It can be calculated using the following calibration equation:
(1)
Y = 78.36 X + 71.57

The correlation coefficient (R2) = 0.9908, in the range 0.5–15%.
(2)
Y = 28.64 X + 841.89
The correlation coefficient (R2) = 0.9924, in the range of 15–100%.
Under the equation that LOD = 3σ/k, the LOD is reduced to 0.0895%. (σ is standard deviation of the blank signal with 11 parallel measurements and ks is the slope of the regression line).

For the peak position shift strategy, the optimum fluorescence peak position of o-CDs red-shifted gradually, which changed as the concentration of water increases. The position of the fluorescence peak red-shifted from 573 to 601 nm. There existed a single linear relationship from 0.5 to 100%, which can be expressed as
(3)
Y = 0.2932 X + 574.71
The correlation coefficient (R2) = 0.9931, the LOD is 3.9729%.

Similarly, the fluorescence emissions were recorded at optimum excitation wavelengths for methanol, dimethylformamide (DMF), and acetone media introduced into water (Fig. S6−S8). The linear ranges for each of them are 0.5–20%, 0.5−70%, and 20–70%, v/v and the corresponding LODs are 1.6213, 0.0804, 0.2182%, v/v, respectively (Table S2). Compared with other CDs-based water sensing methods, o-CDs based sensors have superior sensitivity and wide linear range (Table S3). Hence, it is valuable for o-CDs as fluorescent probes to detect water content in organic solvents.

To further explore the structure and sensing mechanism of o-CDs, some observations and experiments were carried out. Fig. S9a showed TEM images of o-CDs in water. Fig. S9b was the 2D AFM image and Fig. S9c was the 3D height profile AFM image of o-CDs in water. According to the figures above, the o-CDs were evenly dispersed in ethanol. While in water, they had poor solubility and partial aggregation. The Zeta potentials of o-CDs in ethanol and water are −3.11 mV (Fig. S10a) and 5.19 mV (Fig. S10b), indicating that the surface state of o-CDs changed and the interactions between water and o-CDs happened. Due to the low solubility and dispersion of Ds in water, aggregation results in π−π stacking interactions of graphitized nuclei and the excited-state intramolecular proton transfer effect. And with the increase of solvent polarity, the hydration of carbonyl group changes the surface state of o-CDs. Hydrogen bond and other specific water–fluorophore interaction increases the conjugation degree of the o-CDs system, which leads to the decrease of energy gap required for π–π transition and the increase of non-radiative transition probability and the occurrence of the excited-state molecular proton transfer effect. Meanwhile, the fluorescence quantum yield and lifetime changed. These verified speculated mechanism successfully. Therefore, the determination of water content in real samples of the o-CDs system could get good practical application performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water content determination in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to validate potential applications, determination of water content in liquor, medicinal alcohol, and air was implemented. Importantly, we developed a dual-mode sensing strategy of fluorescence intensity and peak position detection. The fluorescence assay was examined to detect water content in two spirit samples such as 42° Shanxi Fen Wine (Table 1 ) and 75% v/v medicinal alcohol (Table S4). The results were comparable to the water volume fraction on the product label and the recovery studies were carried out, indicating great potential of o-CDs for detecting water in the alcohol samples. It could be seen from the table that the recoveries of the two methods were basically consistent. The recoveries of Shanxi Fen Wine were in the range 97.50–114.4% and medicinal alcohol were between 93.20% and 111.1%, with low relative standard deviation.

What is more, the fluorescence paper test strips were prepared by immersed into o-CDs solution and subsequently dried, following 2.4. Then, the orange strips containing o-CDs were made and placed in sealed glass bottles with RH of 11.31%, 33.07%, 59.14%, 67.00%, 85.11%, and 98.00% for 2 days. Next, the fluorescence intensity of the strips was observed under UV lamp (Fig. 6 ). It can be seen that with the increase of air humidity, the orange fluorescence of the strip dimmed gradually. Based on this, we can develop a sensor for immediate on-site detecting water content and design test strips for revealing air humidity in living environment.

In the field of advanced anti-counterfeiting, fluorescent materials have attracted extensive attention because of their low toxicity, functionability, immediate response to external stimuli, and difficulty in replication [34–36]. A lot of luminescent nanomaterials have been designed for information security such as semiconductor quantum dots and organic fluorescent dyes and CDs. Here, we filled the as-prepared o-CDs solution into an empty mark pen and written down “ShanXi University” characters. It appeared pink in the sunlight and orange under 365 nm UV lamp, as shown in Fig. 7 a. When the “han i university” part were covered with acid, it can be seen that the fluorescence disappears under 365 nm. At this very moment, the real content “S X U” emerged to achieve fluorescence anti-counterfeiting successfully. And with alkali solution coated words, the complete information would reappear once again in 365 nm UV lamp.

Furthermore, the filter paper was soaked in o-CDs solution and the orange test paper was prepared (the operation procedure is similar to 2.4). It showed orange fluorescence at 365 nm (Fig. 7b). The surface functional group of the o-CDs cross-linked with the fiber structure of the filter paper. Meanwhile, it bonded with hydroxyl groups in cellulose in the form of hydrogen bonds. The pen filled with acid solution was written on the test paper. It was adelomorphous in sunlight but showed clear blue fluorescence under 365 nm. This is because under acidic conditions (pH = 1–2), there is very weak UV absorption around 360 nm (Fig. S11). Therefore, the orange fluorescence was quenched after acid writing, and the blue fluorescence from the filter paper itself appeared. After soaking the filter paper in alkali solution or coating it completely with alkaline reagent, the blue fluorescent letter disappeared. This was a result of the acid–base neutralization. After drying the test strip, the clear blue words can be written again with acid reagent to realize the adelomorphous repetitive writing function of the test paper. And with alkali cover part of the words can also achieve information encryption and anti-counterfeiting functions.

To explore the applicability as fluorescent flexible films, o-CDs were composited with PVA to investigate the optical properties. Fig. S12a showed the fluorescence photos under sunlight and UV light at different concentrations. Because of the different binding degree of o-CDs surface functional group and hydroxyl group in PVA, the fluorescence intensity varied with o-CDs concentration. As you can see in Fig. S12b, the properties of the PVA films also were altered due to the pH difference. The fluorescence color of the acidic o-CDs/PVA films was red under 365 nm. In this process, the protonation and deprotonation would happen. These further confirmed their applications in solid-state fluorescent materials and information encryption.

In summary, we have developed a feasible scheme for synthesizing o-CDs with orange emission. We found that the fluorescence intensity and peak position was directly relevant to the concentration of water. The possible detection mechanism is that the hydration of between water and fluorophore induced aggregation of the o-CDs system, which increases the conjugation degree and reduces the energy level transition probability. From this, we developed a dual determination approach of water content in organic solvents and made test strips for air humidity detection. Moreover, a series of anti-counterfeit and encryption experiment was carried out. Environmental conditions (pH) as stimulus signals, o-CDs can be made fluorescent ink, repeatable writing test paper and o-CDs/PVA flexible film for unique information anti-counterfeiting and solid-state luminescent materials.
Yating Meng: Analyzed the data, Resources, Writing-original draft,
Writing-review & editing.
Sai Cui: Investigation, Discussed the results.
Peng Lei:Supervision.
Jianhua Guo:Supervision.
Qi Wang:Analyzed the data,
Shaomin Shuang: Supervision.
Chuan Dong: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274090), the Graduate Student Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (No. 2021Y036).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2023.101669.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: General strategy for in situ generation of a coumarin-Cu(2+) complex for fluorescent water sensing
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Cheng, Y. Xie, Z. Yang, Y. Sun, M.Z. Zhang, Y. Ding, W. Zhang, General Strategy for in Situ Generation of a Coumarin-Cu(2+) Complex for Fluorescent Water Sensing, Anal Chem 91(9) (2019) 5817-5823.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical sensors for determination of water in the organic solvents: a review
J. Iran. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Jouyban, E. Rahimpour, Optical sensors for determination of water in the organic solvents: a review, Journal of the Iranian Chemical Society 19(1) (2021) 1-22.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of trace water content in petroleum and petroleum products
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.A. Frink, D.W. Armstrong, Determination of Trace Water Content in Petroleum and Petroleum Products, Anal Chem 88(16) (2016) 8194-8201.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The utilisation of two detectors for the determination of water in honey using headspace gas chromatography
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.A. Frink, D.W. Armstrong, The utilisation of two detectors for the determination of water in honey using headspace gas chromatography, Food Chem 205 (2016) 23-27.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of water content in alcohol solvents over Al3+ induced colorimetric and NIR-fluorescent sensor based on isophorone-phenylamine
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Erdemir, S. Malkondu, Detection of water content in alcohol solvents over Al3+ induced colorimetric and NIR-fluorescent sensor based on isophorone-phenylamine, Microchemical Journal 160 (2021) 105677.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Some aspects of the determination of water by the karl Fisher method
J. Anal. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual photoluminescence emission carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent GSH sensing and cancer cell recognition
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Li, L. Shi, J. Jia, O. Eltayeb, W. Lu, Y. Tang, C. Dong, S. Shuang, Dual Photoluminescence Emission Carbon Dots for Ratiometric Fluorescent GSH Sensing and Cancer Cell Recognition, ACS Appl Mater Interfaces 12(16) (2020) 18250-18257.
